So far, it has not. For example, Meta has only seen $3 to $5 billion of incremental revenue from $70 billion in AI capital expenditures. Generously assuming 100% margins on that revenue, that is only a 4% to 7% ROI, which is not much better than T-bills.

ROIs on AI spending need to come quickly, as AI hardware has an estimated useful life of only two to three years. In contrast, the fiber optics laid during the late 1990s tech bubble had decades of useful life.

According to consulting firm Bain & Co., AI revenue needs to reach a whopping $2 trillion in 2030 to justify all of this AI capex. That is more than the combined revenue of Amazon, Apple, Google, Meta, Microsoft, and Nvidia. AI revenue last year was only $45 billion. Thus, AI revenue needs to skyrocket more than 40-fold over the next five years, or else Wall Street will be very disappointed. Talk about a big bet on a new technology!

Weakening Economic Data

Despite all this AI capex, the latest consumer, manufacturing, and employment data suggest the economy is weakening.

Consumer Sentiment Is Near All-Time Lows

Consumer spending is important, to say the least, since it is about two-thirds of GDP. The latest University of Michigan consumer survey showed consumer sentiment fell 2.6 points to 51 in November. That was the fourth consecutive monthly decline and below expectations of 53. It was the second lowest level in the past 65 years, as this chart shows. It was as low as it was during the terrible “stagflationary" early 1980s and even lower than during the Global Financial Crisis of 2008-2009. Imagine how low it would be if we were in a recession with much higher unemployment and a stock bear market.

Consumer sentiment about current economic conditions fell 6.3 points to the lowest level in history. That is even lower than in 2022, when inflation was much higher and most economists and investors were expecting a recession.

Similarly, consumer expectations about the future fell 1.3 points to one of the lowest levels in history.

The University of Michigan survey also showed that 44% of middle-income consumers said their financial situation is worse now than it was a year ago. That is nearly double the 23% who said it was better, as shown below. The primary reason consumers cited for their worsening financial situation is continuing inflation. And now the Fed wants to print more money and put more fuel on the inflation fire? Why would they want to do that? Maybe because the economy is clearly weakening.

Consumers are also worried about losing their jobs. As this chart shows, job loss expectations are reaching new highs, particularly for young consumers.

Indeed, consumer expectations for higher unemployment are as high now as they were in the three worst recessions in the past 65 years. Incredible. Maybe this is catching the Fed's attention and spurring them to create more money out of thin air.

Historically, the S&P 500 has been correlated with consumer sentiment. But with the aid of AI mania, the stock market (red line) has continued to rise while consumer sentiment (black line) has plummeted. Never before has Wall Street been so disconnected from Main Street. More Fed money creation will likely only exacerbate this situation.

Manufacturing Activity Is Contracting

Since manufacturing is a highly cyclical industry, it is a leading indicator for the economy. Unfortunately, the ISM Manufacturing Purchasing Managers Index (“PMI”) for November showed contraction in manufacturing, new orders, and employment. It also showed prices continue to rise, which will not help consumers trying to deal with the massive increase in living costs in recent years.

The table below shows the overall PMI fell slightly to 48.2. That is below the neutral 50 level, which means manufacturing is contracting rather than expanding. The new orders and employment indexes both dropped 2 points to 47.4 and 44, respectively. Backlogs fell 3.9 points to 44. The price index ticked up to 58.5. These are "stagflationary" numbers and do not bode well for the economic outlook.

Employment Is Worsening

Due to the “shutdown” of the US Federal government (but not "shutdown" enough to stop collecting taxes, of course), the Bureau of Labor Statistics released the September employment report seven weeks after it was due.

According to this late report, 119K new jobs were added in September. That was higher than consensus expectations and a significant increase from the 22K new jobs initially reported for August.

Unfortunately, as has been the pattern with the government jobs data, there were significant negative revisions to prior jobs numbers. Payrolls for July and August had negative revisions of 7K and 26K jobs, respectively. In fact, August was revised down from +22K to -4K, which means jobs actually declined in August, which is typically a sign of recession. Given the pattern of negative jobs revisions, we will take the September "upside surprise" with a grain of salt for now.

Another concerning fact from the September jobs report is that the growth in overall employment ticked down slightly from +0.9% in August to +0.83% in September. Based on historical data, after the three-month percentage change in employment nears zero or turns negative, a recession has occurred. For example, in December 2000, employment growth slowed to just +0.28%. That was followed by a recession and the tech bust, when the NASDAQ fell over 80%. Then in December 2007, employment growth slowed even more to only +0.21%. That was the month the Great Recession started, which was the worst recession since the Great Depression, and it caused the S&P 500 to fall more than 50%. Based on the September data, the three-month percentage change in employment was only 0.117%, as the chart below shows. That is even slower than it was before the early 2000s and 2008-2009 recessions. That is not encouraging.

Another concerning fact from the jobs report is the increase in the unemployment rate from 4.3% in August to 4.4% in September. Whenever the unemployment rate has increased by at least 0.5% from its cycle lows, a recession has occurred historically. Since the cycle lows in 2023, the unemployment rate has increased by 1%, double the historical recession signal.

Apparently, the government will likely not release an October jobs report, and we will have to wait until December 16 for the November jobs report.

Fortunately, we have other employment data to monitor for clues about the state of the jobs market. The chart below shows weekly initial unemployment claims (blue line) have only risen 8.6% from their 2022 lows. However, continuing unemployment claims have risen 43% from their 2022 lows (red line). Such a massive increase in continuing jobless claims has been a recession sign in the past.

In addition to the limited government data we have now, private data sources also show employment is worsening.

For example, the private payrolls report from ADP is highly regarded and correlates well historically with the government data on private payrolls (particularly after government revisions). Unfortunately, ADP reported that private payrolls fell by -32K jobs in November, below the consensus estimate of +10K, as shown below. The three-month moving average is negative 4K, which is the first three-month decline since the 2020 lockdown-driven economic collapse. A year ago, the three-month average was about +200K. This is a significant deterioration. A decline in private payrolls is definitely not a bullish sign.

The ADP report also showed small businesses cut a massive -120K jobs, which is the sixth decline in small business jobs in the past seven months. Small businesses account for nearly half of all employment, and they are clearly struggling under the burden of stubbornly high inflation and interest rates. Dr. Nela Richardson, chief economist at ADP, summarized the report as follows:

”Hiring has been choppy of late as employers weather cautious consumers and an uncertain macroeconomic environment. And while November's slowdown was broad-based, it was led by a pullback among small businesses."

Speaking of small businesses, the percentage of small firms that say poor sales are their most important problem has increased to more than 10%, according to the recent NFIB small business survey. The chart below shows how highly correlated that percentage (green line) is with the US unemployment rate (black line). This suggests the unemployment rate is likely to rise further in the months ahead.

In addition to the lagged effects of the Fed's tight monetary policy, AI appears to be hurting employment. For example, ADP shows IT jobs have been declining since ChatGPT was released in late 2022. The loss of these high-income jobs is one reason consumer sentiment is so weak.

This chart from the Chicago Fed shows that layoffs have risen steadily since the Fed started raising rates and AI adoption has increased.

This is also true for the hiring rate, as shown below. Clearly, struggling companies are reluctant to hire, particularly with the availability of new productivity-enhancing AI tools.

All of this is leading to the biggest increase in layoffs since the Great Recession. Challenger layoff data showed October had the most job cuts of any October in more than two decades. The chart below estimates the annualized number of layoffs in 2025 by assuming November and December are the year-to-date average. As Challenger explained, ”this comes as AI adoption, softening consumer and corporate spending, and rising costs drive belt-tightening and hiring freezes.”

Is Bitcoin Leading Stocks Into A Bear Market?

As we have discussed in this Seeking Alpha article, Bitcoin is highly correlated with stocks and often leads stocks in major trend changes, as it did in late 2021 and early 2025. This chart shows Bitcoin (black line) has fallen out of bed lately. Will the NASDAQ 100 (red line) follow? If the economic data continues to weaken, we think so.

Conclusions

The AI boom has been keeping the economy and stock market going in recent years, but it appears increasingly unsustainable. Weak consumer sentiment, manufacturing activity, and employment data are not encouraging for the economic outlook or for risk-on assets like Bitcoin and stocks. Most investors are ignoring this for now. Further weakness in Bitcoin and tech stocks may cause them to start looking for reasons for the weakness. I hope this article will help investors searching for an understanding of the state of the economy. I look forward to reading and learning from your comments.

data.title: Making Sense Of Noisy Jobs Data
data.description:
The job market has been less than stellar recently. While it seems nearly everyone can agree that it's trending in the wrong direction, and that's a problem, few can agree on the cause of the problem or the solution.

I still believe that AI is a major culprit and that software developers in general, junior developers in particular, have been the hardest hit by the introduction of cheap large language models ("LLMs").

We can see this trend in tech employment, although many other sectors look like this too. It's just especially pronounced in tech.

Overall, we haven't seen net job losses in the economy, but it's slowing in both private and public data. The last few months have been showing that the economy is in a "low hire, low fire" environment. The latest monthly private payrolls report from ADP Research (ADP) continued this downward trend.

This is consistent with my view that the Fed funds rate being held for most of this year, when they were expected to lower it, has thrown off many businesses, especially smaller businesses. After falling into this year, what many expected would be a continuation turned into a 9-month hold before cuts resumed.

Add in policy uncertainty from Washington, throwing significant amounts of trade into question, and you have a recipe for many businesses to be cautious about increasing their largest, stickiest expense: people.

Divergence Between Large and Small Firms

When we break down the ADP data, we see something that I've been concerned about and discussing since it started earlier this year: small and large payrolls are bifurcating. While small and medium-sized businesses have been announcing layoffs and reducing payrolls, large businesses continue to hire in aggregate.

The issue is that the majority of Americans are employed by these small and medium-sized firms, and so the overall trend in jobs has stagnated since Q1 this year, even as large firms keep hiring.

When looking at the by-firm-size results for November, losses in the small firms outpaced the gains made by large firms by 32,000 jobs. November ended at a net loss.

The paradigm shift is easiest to see in a longer-term chart when viewing the lower end of mid-sized firms against large firms. These categories employ a similar total amount of workers. While they had roughly even gains in November, this hasn't been the norm over the last few years.

Few Data Sources For the Fed to Pull From

Private data like ADP is important because the disruption to the BLS' data, brought on by the historic government shutdown this year, is still ongoing. November's job data from the BLS is being delayed to Dec. 16th, up from Dec. 5. This puts the release on the other side of the Federal Reserve's Dec. 10th FOMC meeting to determine rate policy.

The Fed will be flying blind unless they use alternative data, and so they will likely be turning to ADP and other sources. The labor market and its cooling have been a hot-button topic for the Fed, and are the reason that I am expecting a rate cut at this next meeting.

This cooling, not just in small firms netting job losses through H2, but in the slowdown of large firm hiring through H1 of this year, has led to a rising unemployment rate.

The current data is delayed, and we won't know the current BLS unemployment rate until after the FOMC meeting. However, the Chicago Fed does a "nowcast" that, while somewhat noisy at times, has proven a useful guide during the shutdown. It is still useful to see the likely trajectory while we wait for BLS data.

While the rate is now officially above 4.4% at the BLS, the Chicago Fed Nowcast is predicting that we will see a leveling out of the rate, and that we shouldn't continue up to 4.5% in November.

Beware Noisy Labor Data

Beware of alternative data. While I am not a believer in the conspiracies about the BLS, I am skeptical of many datasets, and in particular noisy and potentially manipulated data. Infamously, alternative jobs data like online job postings can be very noisy due to "ghost jobs," or job openings posted by firms for show -- to impress investors, appear like they are growing, etc. -- but rarely materialize into actual people hired.

So consider data like job postings on Indeed (OTCPK:RCRRF), which are up YoY and recently spiked this past month, to be too noisy to be telling of the direction of the general labor market. It is interesting to see but potentially misleading.

Another noisy dataset that should be taken lightly, Google (GOOG) search trends for "File for Unemployment" in the United States. This figure is up, although it broke away from following the trend in initial claims right around the time hiring started to level off in H1 of this year.

I'm sure the timing is no coincidence, but I'm unsure of why this divergence is happening. My first suspect is gig work, where folks who are laid off search for how to file, find out how low the payments are (compared to their old salary), and then end up driving for Uber (UBER), etc., because it pays more.

Of course, there is another explanation that is more in line with the "American spirit" narrative: that folks are losing their jobs and turning to DoorDash (DASH) en masse. Americans are also forming new companies at a historic rate, at an even higher rate today than they were in 2020-2021 when helicopter money (stimulus checks, PPP loans, etc.) was being handed out and rates were cheap, meaning borrowing for startup costs was much cheaper.

The higher rate environment isn't stopping us from starting more businesses, but this entrepreneurship rate seems to have an inverse relationship with the hiring rate. While I am eager to cheer on self-starter businesses, I have to concede that it also comes at a historically low hiring rate.

After all, more businesses doesn't mean there will be more successful businesses. Maybe that's too skeptical of a take -- I am rooting for self-starters (I'm one of them!), and I'm on record for saying that there were few times in history that it was easier to build something yourself and give it a go.

Conclusion

The labor market is unmistakably continuing to cool into November, and despite a delay in official BLS data to confirm this, the Fed has access to enough alternative data to see this too. The plan, as I anticipate it, likely remains unchanged. We will see another 25bp cut at the Dec. 10 FOMC meeting, as the Fed works to counteract this cooling.

I expect the rhetoric around this decision to sound prudent and firm; if I were a gambler, I'd bet on the phrase "risk management cut" being said at the press conference after the meeting.

We're starting to leave the "low fire, low hire" job market and enter the "high fire, low hire" market, but mostly for small and mid-sized firms. This needs immediate addressing before it gets worse, and the bifurcation between large and small firms gets worse. These large companies have more resources to weather high borrowing costs and low consumer sentiment. The little guys, less so. The longer it goes on, the worse it will be for them, and the greater I expect the gap to become.

Thanks for reading.
